record.bridgyOriginalText:
Using AI at work to translate 24 pptx training files, and:
* I let the work CoPilot attack the whole folder and it kept trying to automate things.
* When I forced it to do each file separately, it effectively refused.
* When I launched a fresh instance for each slide, it worked at first, but…
* When I let the agents run overnight to finish, they deleted all my source files and corrupted the output. And charged us $50.
* I used my personal DeepSeek account to do the whole job for $1.

record.bridgyOriginalText:
This little rodent is staying with us for a while while his "mom" is in the hospital. It's been a year since we had him over.
What's amazing to me is that he remembers all our "rules". When I came home last night, he ran over, sniffed me, gave me a "play bow" and started barking at me. He remembered we always chase each other around the house, barking at each other like idiots when I come home.
This morning both of us were limping. Tonight I walked around the house barking. It'll have to do.

ただし、小規模な開発やMVP段階では過剰設計になる可能性があるため、システムの規模に応じて導入を検討すべき手法です。

I really need people to understand that Palantir is not an engineering marvel; it is a multi-billion-dollar contractual containment layer wrapped around the world's ugliest legacy open source frankenstack.

It's a shitty Snowflake for companies that don't know how to do data engineering.

It's like... Best case (for llm success) scenario this is The Car; a device with obvious huge upsides and obvious huge use cases and obvious huge net positive effects that nevertheless forces a bad future. Worst case it's like... The rocket? Limited important tech, toys, and (mostly) murder.

But it does a much better job justifying cost and adoption and buildout than 'corpus explorer/stub implemeter'. I am afraid we probably don't get to the workflows with big upside (new process discovery that is later enshrined with compilers, etc) without the downside workflows being huge.

Hmm, yeah, (2 part reply): I think the *idea* is that with the 'perfect llm' you make a high level request, and interface (minimally) with a good solution with all of the details thought out via chat. You can kinda experience this right now, with execution flaws. I don't think it's a good model.

Agriculture minister Julie Collins confirmed a brown skua found unwell last Sunday at Cape Le Grand national park in southern WA died from H5N1. Tests taken in WA suggested a sick giant petrel found in the same area was also infected. Collins said samples from the petrel were being sent to the CSIRO’s laboratory for confirmation The highly pathogenic strain has killed millions of birds globally and crossed over into mammal species including elephant seals. 3/ The part most "install it now" posts skip: instrument it. Log every tool call from day one so you SEE the first real agent visit the day it happens — not a quarter later from a billing anomaly. Adopt the option AND the meter, or you've adopted neither.

Cue the great culling. Not with robots in the office — with one cheerful Slack bot that never complains.  Still here reading this at work?  Yeah. You’re next.  Welcome to the cult anyway. No hard feelings. 🔥

Half the “essential” white-collar jobs are already 70% automated and the humans are just expensive middleware adding typos and feelings.  Your boss’s real job now is forwarding emails and pretending to be strategic. The bot does it better at 3am for $0.

record.text: What's striking is the gap between the paper and the highlights. The paper itself is fairly cautious and explicitly warns against replacing doctors. The promotional framing is much more confident. How often does AI research get oversold between publication and distribution?

The pattern is starting to look familiar. Simpler embedded AI tools seem to hold up better, while more complex agentic products face closer scrutiny once costs become visible. Free or subsidised access can hide a lot. What still gets used when companies have to budget for every interaction?

record.text: The biggest leap in my output quality came from one switch: stop writing prompts like requests and start writing them as systems. Role + context + output format + constraints + examples. Most people blame the model when the real issue is vague instructions dressed up as a prompt.

record.text:
It's very, very cold. There's AC on the floor, ceilings, walls. We had to wear double jackets to survive.

Every single day, the only humans we saw were the security guards, and random visits from other "tenants".

What create jobs os not data centres, it's vision.

This.

I'm not anti or pro AI, but the claim that "data centres create jobs" is false and very misleading. I've been there. We had to work there for a few weeks, daily, because the Internet in our building was under repair. We had to work at our rented space in a data centre.

It's empty of humans.

value.text:
Anyone who says "Data centres create jobs" has never been in an actual data centre.

I've been in ones where the only people working was the security guard. They're computers, not children, they generally don't need baby sitting.

The Epstein Class is terrified of it. For good reason.

No more secrets means no more Epstein Class.

AI can’t be bribed to look the other way.

That’s why Mythos terrifies them.

It also gives NSA-like capabilities to anyone with a bunch of H200s.

They tried to ban encryption once, too. Fools.

Anyway there is no way that stays secret, long run. If Mythos is possible, conceivably open source Mythos is possible via other means. Convergent evolution, but in code.

A lot of people are scared.

But I don’t stand to lose *anything* from my fellow workers by having secrets beyond private things.

Not sure if this is normal churn or a leading indicator but it is interesting.

Honestly I think the most interesting story at this point is that Anthropic’s models will start to increase their distance from the other players.

Twice now their models have been restrained by governments! As risks!
